whois.domain_status.title: Domain Status
whois.ns.title: Nameservers

whois.registry.title: Registrar & Registry
whois.registry.hint: Different TLDs may have different WHOIS fields. Some registries recommend RDAP for structured registration data.
whois.raw.title: Raw Query Results

whois.batch.title: Batch WHOIS Lookup
whois.batch.description: Enter one domain per line. The system will query registrar, registration date, expiry date and domain status for each.
whois.availability.title: Domain Availability
whois.availability.hint: WHOIS results cannot accurately determine domain availability across all TLDs. Final availability should be confirmed with a domain registrar.
whois.privacy.title: About WHOIS Query Results
whois.privacy.body: WHOIS data comes from domain registries, registrars or related public lookup services. Due to privacy protection, GDPR, registrar policies and different TLD rules, some registrant information may be hidden, redacted or not publicly disclosed. Results are suitable for domain management, registration verification, DNS troubleshooting and security analysis reference, but should not be used for harassment, tracking individuals or privacy violation.
whois.privacy.supplement: Different TLDs may have different WHOIS field formats. Some domains may be better queried via RDAP for structured information.
whois.faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
whois.faq.a1: WHOIS is a system for querying domain registration information. It typically shows registrar, registration date, expiry, domain status and nameservers.
whois.faq.q2: Can WHOIS show the domain owner?
whois.faq.a2: Not necessarily. Many domains use privacy protection, or registrant details may be hidden due to registrar and regional privacy policies.
whois.faq.a3: Common reasons include privacy protection services, GDPR, registrar policies, registry rules, or the domain owner actively hiding contact information.
whois.faq.q4: What is the difference between WHOIS and RDAP?
whois.faq.a4: WHOIS usually returns plain text results, while RDAP returns structured JSON data. Some registries recommend RDAP.
whois.faq.q5: Does this tool save my query history?
whois.faq.a5: This tool only uses your query for immediate display. We do not actively save the domains you enter.

whois.guide.hero.title: WHOIS Lookup Guide
whois.guide.hero.subtitle: A practical reference on WHOIS queries: what they are, how to read them, and how to use domain registration data for management, SEO and security analysis.
whois.guide.toc.title: Table of Contents
whois.guide.toc.s3: How to query domain registration information
whois.guide.s1.title: What is WHOIS
whois.guide.s1.p1: WHOIS is a query and response protocol used to query databases that store information about registered domain names, IP address allocations and autonomous systems. It has been a fundamental part of the internet infrastructure since the early days of ARPANET.
whois.guide.s1.p2: When you register a domain, the registrar collects technical and administrative contact information and makes portions of it available through WHOIS lookups. This transparency helps with network coordination, troubleshooting and accountability.
whois.guide.s2.title: What information can WHOIS show
whois.guide.s2.p1: A typical WHOIS query returns registrar information, creation and expiration dates, domain status codes, and nameserver assignments. Some queries also reveal registrant organization, country and abuse contact details.
whois.guide.s2.p2: The exact fields available depend on the top-level domain (TLD), the registrar's policies, and whether the domain owner has enabled privacy protection. Not all fields are publicly visible for all domains.
whois.guide.s3.title: How to query domain registration information
whois.guide.s3.p1: Enter the domain name (without http:// or https://) into a WHOIS lookup tool and submit the query. The tool contacts the appropriate registry or registrar and returns the registration record.
whois.guide.s3.p2: For best results, use the root domain (example.com) rather than subdomains (www.example.com). Some tools support IDN (internationalized domain names) and automatically convert them to Punycode.
whois.guide.s4.title: Registrar, Registry and Registrant
whois.guide.s4.p1: The registrant is the person or organization that owns the domain. The registrar is the company where the domain was registered (e.g., GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare). The registry is the organization that manages the TLD (e.g., Verisign for .com, CNNIC for .cn).
whois.guide.s4.p2: When you buy a domain, you interact with the registrar, which in turn communicates with the registry. The registry maintains the master database for that TLD and sets the rules for registration.
whois.guide.s5.title: Registration dates explained
whois.guide.s5.p1: The creation date is when the domain was first registered. The updated date is the last time the registration record was modified. The expiration date is when the current registration period ends, after which the domain may enter a grace period, redemption period, and eventually become available again.
whois.guide.s5.p2: These dates are critical for domain portfolio management, renewal planning, and assessing the age of a domain for SEO purposes. Older domains often carry more trust in search engine algorithms.
whois.guide.s6.title: Common domain status codes
whois.guide.s6.p1: Domain status codes indicate what operations are allowed or restricted on a domain. Common codes include: clientTransferProhibited (prevents unauthorized transfers), clientUpdateProhibited (prevents modification), clientDeleteProhibited (prevents deletion), and serverTransferProhibited (registry-level restriction).
whois.guide.s6.p2: These codes are set by the registrar or registry to protect domains from accidental or malicious changes. If you need to transfer a domain, you may need to contact your registrar to remove certain status codes first.
whois.guide.s7.title: What are Nameservers
whois.guide.s7.p1: Nameservers (NS) are DNS servers that store the DNS records for a domain. When someone visits your domain, their device asks the nameserver for the corresponding IP address. Changing nameservers switches which DNS provider manages your domain's records.
whois.guide.s7.p2: Common nameserver patterns include those from Cloudflare, Google Cloud DNS, Amazon Route 53, and registrar-provided DNS. After changing nameservers, global propagation can take from minutes to 48 hours.
whois.guide.s8.title: WHOIS privacy protection
whois.guide.s8.p1: WHOIS privacy protection (also called domain privacy or ID protection) replaces the registrant's personal contact information in the public WHOIS record with proxy information provided by the privacy service.
whois.guide.s8.p2: This protects domain owners from spam, harassment, and identity theft. After GDPR came into effect in 2018, many European registrars began redacting personal data by default, making privacy protection even more common.
whois.guide.s9.title: Why owner information is hidden
whois.guide.s9.p1: Beyond voluntary privacy services, regulations like GDPR in Europe and various national data protection laws require registrars to limit the public disclosure of personal information. Some registries also have policies that hide certain fields.
whois.guide.s9.p2: If you need to contact a domain owner for legitimate purposes (such as trademark disputes), you can often use the registrar's contact form or the privacy service's forwarding email, if available.
whois.guide.s10.title: WHOIS vs RDAP
whois.guide.s10.p1: RDAP (Registration Data Access Protocol) is the modern successor to WHOIS. It returns structured JSON data instead of unstructured plain text, supports internationalization, authentication, and standardized error handling.
whois.guide.s10.p2: Many registries now support RDAP and some have deprecated traditional WHOIS port-43 queries. RDAP makes it easier for automated systems to parse registration data reliably.
whois.guide.s11.title: Use cases
whois.guide.s11.p1: WHOIS data is used for domain portfolio management, competitive analysis, security investigations, trademark monitoring, technical troubleshooting, and SEO research. Knowing when a competitor's domain expires or which registrar they use can inform business decisions.
whois.guide.s11.p2: Security professionals use WHOIS to investigate phishing domains, report abuse to registrars, and map infrastructure. Network administrators use it to find abuse contacts for IP ranges.
whois.guide.s12.title: Common mistakes
whois.guide.s12.p1: A common mistake is assuming WHOIS always shows the real owner. With privacy protection and GDPR redaction, the public record often shows a proxy. Another mistake is relying on WHOIS for real-time availability checking — some TLDs have delayed or incomplete data.
whois.guide.s12.p2: People also sometimes confuse the registrar with the registry, or assume that all TLDs have identical field formats. In reality, each registry sets its own data policies and field availability.
whois.guide.s13.title: Frequently Asked Questions
whois.guide.s13.p1: Our WHOIS lookup tool queries public registration databases and presents the results in a structured, readable format. We do not store your queries or share them with third parties.
whois.guide.s13.p2: If you encounter a domain with no WHOIS results, it may be unregistered, use a private registry, or the TLD may not support public WHOIS queries. Try RDAP for more structured data when available.
whois.guide.s14.title: Try the WHOIS Lookup Tool
whois.guide.s14.p1: Ready to look up a domain? Use our WHOIS lookup tool to instantly query registrar information, registration dates, domain status, nameservers and raw WHOIS data.
whois.guide.s14.p2: The tool supports both structured and raw views, handles IDN domains, and provides batch lookup for up to multiple domains at once.
